planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial to full shade in spring or fall; Japanese plum yew tolerates deep shade and competing tree roots better than almost any other broadleaf or needle evergreen shrub.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial to full shade; Japanese plum yew is the single most versatile and practically valuable shade-tolerant evergreen shrub for southeastern and Gulf Coast gardens — producing the same dense, refined, dark-green, yew-like needle texture and appearance as English yew (Taxus baccata) or Japanese yew (T. cuspidata) while tolerating the summer heat, humidity, and root competition of zone 6–9 gardens far better than true yews. Most importantly, unlike all Taxus yews, Cephalotaxus is NOT browsed by deer — an enormous advantage in areas where deer pressure makes growing Taxus impossible. The plum-sized, fleshy, olive-green fruits on female plants are technically edible (alkaloid-free, unlike Taxus berries) but are rarely consumed. Low maintenance, slow-growing, and long-lived. 'Fastigiata' (upright, columnar), 'Prostrata' (low, wide-spreading), and 'Duke Gardens' (compact, spreading, deep shade) are the primary cultivar selections. An absolutely essential plant for the shaded landscape in zones 6–9 where traditional yews fail.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; podocarpus tolerates drought and coastal salt spray well once established and is one of the most versatile and low-maintenance evergreen shrubs for Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; yew podocarpus (Buddhist pine, shrubby podocarpus) is one of the most widely planted evergreen trees and hedge shrubs throughout the Gulf Coast and southeastern United States, prized for its long, flat, dark-green, yew-like leaves on densely branching upright stems that create an elegant, refined, and formally refined appearance whether grown as a specimen tree, a clipped hedge, a privacy screen, or a foundation shrub. Unlike most conifers, podocarpus tolerates significant shade, clay soil, urban heat, salt spray, and drought, making it one of the most adaptable broadleaf conifers available for challenging southeastern growing conditions. It is slower-growing than many hedging plants but ultimately more durable. Left unclipped, it grows eventually to 40 feet as a narrow columnar tree; clipped regularly it maintains any desired height. The small, fleshy, red-purple receptacles bearing the seeds are attractive to birds. Virtually pest-free in the Gulf Coast South.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens or offsets in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to average soil in partial to full shade after frost; coontie is extremely drought-tolerant once established and requires virtually no irrigation after its first growing season — one of the most drought-tolerant native shade plants available for zone 8–11.

directions: Plant container specimens or offset divisions in well-drained, sandy to average soil in partial to full shade; coontie is the only cycad native to the continental United States — a living fossil that has been growing unchanged in Florida and the southeastern coastal plain for millions of years. In the landscape it forms a handsome, low-growing rosette of dark-green, stiff, pinnate, fern-like leaves with narrow, pointed leaflets that create an attractive, tropical-textured groundcover in shaded areas. It is critically important as the sole larval host plant for the Atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala), a brilliantly colored Florida native butterfly (black wings with iridescent blue spots and a red abdomen) that was once thought to be extinct due to over-harvesting of coontie for starch production; the recovery of Atala populations has been directly linked to the increase in coontie cultivation in south Florida landscapes. The large, underground root mass stores starch that was harvested and processed by the Seminole, Calusa, and other Florida indigenous peoples for food. Extremely drought-tolerant once established; virtually maintenance-free.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade after frost; yellow alder self-seeds prolifically in Gulf Coast gardens and will naturalize as a persistent, freely self-maintaining annual or short-lived perennial once established.

directions: Direct-sow or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow alder (West Indian holly, mariposa, butter daisy, ramgoat dashalong) is a Caribbean native tropical shrub that has naturalized throughout south Florida, the Gulf Coast, and tropical landscapes worldwide, producing bright, clear-yellow, five-petaled flowers with dark-yellow centers on a continuous, self-seeding basis from late spring through frost. Individual flowers are open only in the morning hours, closing by noon, but the plant produces such a continuous succession of buds that the overall display appears constant throughout the warm season. It grows rapidly from seed, blooms within weeks of germination, and self-seeds with such vigor in suitable warm climates that it functions essentially as a permanently self-maintaining annual or short-lived perennial groundcover once introduced to a sunny area. Deer-resistant, drought-tolerant, and heat-adapted. Useful as a low-maintenance color plant for hot, dry areas and disturbed ground in zone 9–12 gardens.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in cool spring or fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; common mallow self-seeds freely and will establish self-maintaining colonies in suitable conditions — one spring sowing typically ensures perpetual renewal.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in cool spring or fall in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; common mallow is a biennial or short-lived perennial edible herb of European origin that has been used as food and medicine for thousands of years — the young leaves, fresh or cooked, have a mild, slightly mucilaginous, pleasantly neutral flavor and can be used in salads, cooked as a pot herb (substitute for spinach), or simmered in soups and stews. The leaves, stems, seeds (called 'cheeses' for their round shape), and flowers are all edible. The plant has long been valued in herbal medicine for its mucilaginous properties, which soothe irritated tissues of the throat, digestive tract, and urinary system. The attractive, pale to deep-pink or purple flowers with darker veining are produced throughout the growing season and are excellent in salads. The ornamental cultivar 'Zebrina' with striped purple-and-white flowers and 'Brave Heart' are grown primarily for their striking flowers. Tolerates partial shade, poor soil, and drought once established. Self-seeds freely to maintain persistent garden colonies.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
